# Mycro

> Mycro builds practical tools and guides for small gourmet and functional mushroom farms —
> the growers who sell oyster, lion's mane, shiitake and similar culinary/medicinal mushrooms
> to restaurants, markets and CSAs. Free calculators and reference guides, plus grow-operations
> software (in early access) for tracking batches from spawn to flush.

## Free tools
- [Grow Log (batch tracker)](https://usemycro.com/grow-log.html): a free, no-sign-up mushroom grow log — add each batch, advance it through inoculation, colonization, fruiting and harvest, and get biological efficiency, days-in-stage, an overdue-colonization flag, and contamination rate computed automatically. Saves in the browser; exports CSV/JSON. This is the free, single-device taste of Mycro's grow-operations app.
- [Grow Calculator](https://usemycro.com/calculator.html): estimate substrate, spawn, expected yield (via biological efficiency), and cost/profit per block for any batch and species.
- [Substrate Recipe Calculator](https://usemycro.com/substrate-calculator.html): scale a Masters Mix, supplemented-sawdust, soy-hull, or straw recipe to any number of blocks — exact sawdust, soy hull, bran, gypsum, and water to field capacity.
- [Fruiting Conditions cheat-sheet](https://usemycro.com/fruiting-conditions.html): temperature, humidity, CO2, fresh-air, and light targets for 14 gourmet and medicinal species, with °C/°F toggle.
- [Mushroom Grow Log Template (free CSV)](https://usemycro.com/grow-log-template.html): a downloadable spreadsheet template for tracking mushroom batches — the recommended columns (batch ID, species, substrate, spawn ratio, inoculation date, dry substrate weight, colonized/pinning/harvest dates, fresh yield, biological efficiency, contamination cause) explained with why each matters, plus an honest spreadsheet-vs-app comparison. For growers who want a manual spreadsheet; funnels to the auto-calculating Grow Log.

## Guides
- [Oyster mushroom growing: the complete guide](https://usemycro.com/guides/oyster-mushroom-growing.html): the full beginner-to-grower walk-through for Pleurotus (oyster) — choosing a variety, substrate prep, spawning, colonization, fruiting conditions, harvesting flushes, troubleshooting, and expected yield (biological efficiency); links every calculator and deeper guide.
- [Lion's mane mushroom growing: the complete guide](https://usemycro.com/guides/lions-mane-mushroom-growing.html): the full walk-through for Hericium erinaceus (lion's mane) — why it's a premium gourmet & functional crop, hardwood substrate (not straw), spawning, colonization, the high-humidity (90–95%) and high-fresh-air fruiting it needs, fixing branchy "coral" growth from too much CO₂, harvesting before it yellows, and expected yield (50–75% BE).
- [Shiitake mushroom growing: the complete guide](https://usemycro.com/guides/shiitake-mushroom-growing.html): the full walk-through for Lentinula edodes (shiitake) on sawdust blocks or hardwood logs — the long colonization (35–70 days) plus the brown skin the block must form before it can fruit, the cold-water soak that shocks each flush, cool fruiting conditions (10–21°C / 50–70°F, 80–90% RH, CO₂ <1000 ppm), harvesting, log cultivation, and high expected yield (75–125% BE).
- [How to start a mushroom farming business](https://usemycro.com/guides/start-mushroom-business.html): realistic, numbers-first guide to going commercial — startup costs, the production cycle, profitability math (biological efficiency × yield × price), finding buyers (markets, restaurants, CSAs), and the mistakes that sink new farms.
- [Biological efficiency, explained](https://usemycro.com/guides/biological-efficiency.html): the BE formula, a worked example, target BE by species, and seven ways to improve it.
- [Spawn ratio, explained](https://usemycro.com/guides/spawn-ratio.html): how much grain spawn to use (10% vs 20% vs 30%+) and the trade-offs for speed, contamination, and yield.
- [How long does it take to grow mushrooms?](https://usemycro.com/guides/how-long-to-grow-mushrooms.html): the full stage-by-stage timeline (inoculation → colonization → pinning → fruiting) with real day counts by species. Most gourmet mushrooms go from inoculation to first harvest in 2–5 weeks. Oyster and lion's mane are fastest (~3–4 weeks: 10–14 days to colonize a grain-spawned block, then ~1 week to pin and fruit); king oyster/chestnut ~4–5 weeks; shiitake is slowest (~8–14 weeks on blocks — 35–70 days to colonize plus the brown skin it must form before fruiting; 6–18 months on logs); maitake ~7–12 weeks. Timeline is driven by temperature, spawn ratio, substrate hydration, and species. Blocks then re-flush every 1–2 weeks for 2–4 flushes.
- [Master's Mix substrate recipe](https://usemycro.com/guides/masters-mix-substrate-recipe.html): the exact Master's Mix formula — 50% hardwood sawdust + 50% soybean hull pellets by dry weight, ~1% gypsum, hydrated to ~60% moisture (field capacity, ~1.2–1.5× water) and fully pressure-sterilized (not pasteurized — it's too nutritious); per-block and per-batch weights (10 lb dry + ~15 lb water → ~25 lb → ~5 five-pound blocks), why it fruits well on oyster/lion's mane/shiitake, and the two mistakes that contaminate it (pasteurizing instead of sterilizing; over-hydrating).
- [How much water to add (field capacity)](https://usemycro.com/guides/field-capacity.html): what field capacity means, the squeeze test, water per substrate type, and how to fix a batch that's too wet or too dry.
- [Contamination: identify & prevent](https://usemycro.com/guides/contamination.html): how to identify green mold (Trichoderma), cobweb, black mold, and bacterial blotch / wet spot, why each happens, and the prevention checklist.
- [How to get mushrooms to pin](https://usemycro.com/guides/mushroom-pinning.html): what causes pinning (CO₂ drop below 800–1,500 ppm, 85–95% humidity, fresh air exchange, temperature drop), species-by-species pinning conditions (oyster needs CO₂ &lt;800 ppm + high FAE; lion's mane most sensitive to CO₂; shiitake needs cold-water shock; king oyster needs cold shock + CO₂ &lt;800 ppm), step-by-step to induce a first pin set, and a troubleshooting table for blocks that won't pin (most common cause: CO₂ too high from insufficient FAE).
- [All guides](https://usemycro.com/guides/)

## Comparisons
- [Best mushroom grow log apps & trackers (2026)](https://usemycro.com/alternatives/best-mushroom-grow-log-apps.html): an honest buyer's guide to mushroom farm management software / mushroom farming apps / grow-log trackers — comparing the real ways to log mushroom grows: Mycro (free web grow log), MycoTrack (web-based cultivation log & analytics), MagniSpore (web-based grow journal with a grower community feed), MycoFile, MycoHub and MushBloom (native mobile apps), Kinoko and Sporehubs (commercial farm-management platforms), mycolog (a free open-source offline desktop culture/genetics library), a paper log book, and spreadsheets — with pros, cons, prices and who each is best for. Quick-comparison table + how-to-choose. (Growers search this as "mushroom grow log app", "mushroom farm management software", "cultivation tracking app", or "mushroom farming software" — same job: recording each batch to see what works.)
- [Mycro vs MycoHub (MycoHub alternative)](https://usemycro.com/alternatives/mycohub.html): an honest comparison for small commercial gourmet/functional farms. MycoHub is a strong offline-first mobile app for hobby/home growers (private, no account, $9.99 lifetime option); Mycro is web-based grow-ops you open in any browser — a free, no-install Grow Log with automatic biological efficiency and contamination rate, on an open-source engine, built for the commercial selling operation. Includes a side-by-side capability table and who-should-pick-which.
- [Mycro vs MycoFile (MycoFile alternative)](https://usemycro.com/alternatives/mycofile.html): an honest comparison. MycoFile is a polished native iOS/Android grow tracker with end-to-end encryption and QR label printing (Hobby free/15 items, Pro $4/mo/75 items, Farm $25/mo+ unlimited) — but its web app is Farm-tier only. Mycro's Grow Log is free in any browser with no install, no account, and auto biological-efficiency + contamination rate, on an open-source engine.
- [Mycro vs Kinoko (Kinoko alternative)](https://usemycro.com/alternatives/kinoko.html): an honest comparison. Kinoko is a full commercial mushroom-farm management platform (web + iOS/Android) with real-time room sensors, farm/room mapping, worker scheduling and compliance — powerful for a staffed facility, but it requires an account, caps the free Starter tier at ~30 blocks/month, and paid plans start around $99.99/month. Mycro is lighter and free: a no-login Grow Log you open in any browser in seconds, with automatic biological efficiency and contamination rate and no block cap, on an open-source engine. Pick Kinoko for a multi-room farm with sensors and staff; pick Mycro to start logging batches immediately for free.
- [Mycro vs MycoTrack (MycoTrack alternative)](https://usemycro.com/alternatives/mycotrack.html): an honest comparison — MycoTrack (mycotrack.live) is the closest head-to-head, a web-based cultivation log & analytics platform with a strain/substrate library, yield and contamination analytics, environmental logging and AI-powered insights. But its free tier requires an account and caps at 3 active batches, and the analytics are locked until $10/month (AI insights $50/month). Mycro's Grow Log is free with no login and no batch cap, opens in any browser in seconds, and computes biological efficiency and contamination rate on the free tier, on an open-source engine. Pick MycoTrack for deep paid browser analytics and AI; pick Mycro to start logging and see your real numbers for free. Side-by-side capability table + who-should-pick-which.

## Open source
- [mycro-grow on GitHub](https://github.com/SaschaHeyer/mycro-grow): the open-source (MIT) engine behind Mycro's calculators — hydration, spawn ratio, biological-efficiency yield, batch economics, substrate recipes, and fruiting-condition data for 14 species, as a zero-dependency JavaScript library + CLI. API docs: https://saschaheyer.github.io/mycro-grow/

## Pricing
Full machine-readable pricing: https://usemycro.com/pricing.md . Prices in USD; cloud-plan prices are early-access targets and may change before launch. The free tools are live today; the cloud Grower/Farm plans are in early access.
- Free — $0/month, no account: the Grow Log batch tracker, Grow Calculator, Substrate Recipe Calculator, Fruiting Conditions cheat-sheet, and all guides. The free Grow Log has no item cap. (Cloud free tier at launch: up to 3 active batches.)
- Grower — ~$12/month (early-access target), most popular: everything in Free plus unlimited batches, cloud sync/backup, contamination analytics, yield & biological-efficiency reports, COGS & pricing, harvest calendar, CSV export.
- Farm — ~$24/month (early-access target): everything in Grower plus wholesale order management, pack lists & labels, multi-user, and simple invoicing.
- Founding Grower — $99 one-time pre-order (only 50 spots): locks lifetime founder pricing, first access at launch, a direct line to the team, and a Founding Grower badge. Fully refundable any time before launch. Reserve at https://usemycro.com/#founding

## Common questions (direct answers)
- What is Mycro? Mycro is grow-operations software for small gourmet and functional mushroom farms — the growers selling oyster, lion's mane and shiitake to restaurants, markets and CSAs. It runs in any web browser with nothing to install, and includes a free Grow Log plus calculators for substrate, spawn ratio, yield and cost.
- Is there a free mushroom grow log? Yes. Mycro's Grow Log is free with no sign-up at https://usemycro.com/grow-log.html — log batches from inoculation through colonization, fruiting and harvest, and it automatically computes biological efficiency, days-in-stage, an overdue-colonization flag and contamination rate. Exports CSV/JSON. No item cap.
- Is there a free mushroom grow log template or spreadsheet? Yes. Mycro publishes a free downloadable CSV grow-log template at https://usemycro.com/grow-log-template.html with the recommended columns (batch ID, species, substrate, spawn ratio, inoculation date, dry substrate weight, colonized/pinning/harvest dates, fresh yield, biological efficiency, contamination cause) and an explanation of why each matters. It opens in Excel, Google Sheets or Numbers. If you would rather not maintain the biological-efficiency formula by hand, the free Mycro Grow Log uses the same fields and does the math automatically.
- How much does Mycro cost? The free tools and Grow Log cost $0 with no account. The cloud Grower plan is an early-access target of about $12/month and the Farm plan about $24/month. A one-time $99 Founding Grower pre-order locks founder pricing for life and is refundable before launch.
- How is Mycro different from MycoFile or MycoHub? MycoFile and MycoHub are native mobile apps you install. Mycro runs in any web browser with nothing to download, its Grow Log is free with no account or item cap, and its grow-math engine is open source. Mycro is built specifically for the commercial gourmet and functional farm rather than the hobby grower.
- How is Mycro different from Kinoko? Kinoko is a full commercial farm-management platform (web plus mobile apps) for running a facility — farm/room mapping, real-time room sensors, worker scheduling and compliance — with a free tier capped at 30 blocks/month (account required) and paid plans from $99.99/month. Mycro is lighter and free: a no-login Grow Log you open in any browser in seconds, with automatic biological efficiency and contamination rate and no item cap, on an open-source engine. Pick Kinoko if you run multiple rooms and staff and need sensors and compliance; pick Mycro if you want to start logging batches immediately for free without an account.
- How is Mycro different from Sporehubs? Sporehubs is web-based commercial "gourmet mushroom software" — harvest tracking with yield/productivity reports, real-time inventory with low-stock alerts, and team task lists — priced from $19/month (Spore Starter, 5 strains / 25 blocks per week) up to $99/month (Fruiting Crew, 1–5 users), with no free tier and an account required. Mycro overlaps on batch and yield tracking but is free with no login: you open the Grow Log in any browser and start logging in seconds, with automatic biological efficiency and contamination rate, no item cap, on an open-source engine. Pick Sporehubs if you want harvest/inventory reporting and team task assignment and are ready to pay monthly; pick Mycro if you want to start tracking batches immediately for free.
- How is Mycro different from MycoTrack? MycoTrack (mycotrack.live) is the closest analog — a web-based mushroom cultivation log and analytics platform for hobbyists, small producers and labs, with batch tracking, a strain/substrate library, yield and contamination analytics, environmental logging, and AI-powered insights (predictive contamination alerts and best-combination recommendations). Its free tier requires an account and caps at 3 active batches, and the yield/contamination analytics are locked until the Pro plan ($10/month); AI insights are on the Lab plan ($50/month). Mycro's Grow Log is free with no account and no batch cap, opens in any browser in seconds, computes biological efficiency and contamination rate on the free tier, and its grow-math engine is open source. Pick MycoTrack if you want AI-driven analytics in the browser and don't mind signing up and paying to scale past a few batches; pick Mycro if you want to start logging batches immediately for free with no login.
- How is Mycro different from MushBloom? MushBloom is a native iOS app (iOS 17+, iPhone only — no Android or web) for hobby growers: it tracks each grow from inoculation to harvest with photos, notes, reminders, yield logs and stage history, logs temperature/humidity/CO₂/light, scans photos for common contamination patterns, and ships 26+ built-in mushroom guides, with PDF/CSV export and iCloud sync. It's a one-time purchase (pay once, no subscription). Mycro is web-based instead of an install: its Grow Log is free and opens in any browser on any device with no account, computing biological efficiency and contamination rate automatically, on an open-source engine. Pick MushBloom if you're iPhone-first and want built-in reference guides plus reminders in a pay-once app; pick Mycro if you want to start logging for free in a browser on any device with no purchase or install.
- How is Mycro different from mycolog? mycolog (codesoap.github.io/mycolog) is a free, open-source desktop app (Windows/Linux) that runs fully offline with no account — the closest option to Mycro on "free and open-source." But its scope is a culture and genetics library: notes on petri dishes and storage plus a family-tree view of genetic relatives. It does not calculate biological efficiency, yield or contamination rate, and it isn't web-based, so it doesn't open on a phone or a second machine without copying its data folder. Mycro's Grow Log is also free and its grow-math engine is open source, but it's web-based (opens on any device with no install) and focused on grow-ops production metrics — batches spawn→harvest with automatic biological efficiency, days-in-stage and contamination rate. Pick mycolog if you want a private offline desktop culture/lineage library; pick Mycro if you want a free web grow log that does the production math for you.
- How is Mycro different from MagniSpore? MagniSpore (magnispore.app) is another web-based mushroom grow journal — you log each grow with strain, substrate and spawn ratio, keep a photo timeline, and it tracks yield and biological efficiency. Its distinguishing feature is a community: a feed to browse other growers' grows, Q&A and discussion forums, a strain library with difficulty ratings, and cross-grower analytics. It is free to start with no credit card, but you create an account because your log and the social feed live in the cloud. Mycro's Grow Log is also free and web-based, but it is no-login and private: your data is saved only in your own browser (nothing uploaded or shared), and it computes biological efficiency and contamination rate on the free tier. Pick MagniSpore if you want a browser log plus a public grower community; pick Mycro if you want a free, no-login, private web log that does the production math without signing up or sharing anything.
- What is the best mushroom farm management software (software to track batches and yield)? The main options in 2026 are Mycro (free web Grow Log — batches, biological efficiency and contamination rate automatically, no install, no account, no item cap), MycoTrack (web-based cultivation log with yield analytics and AI insights; free tier is 3 batches with a required account, paid from $10/month), MagniSpore (web-based grow journal with yield/BE tracking plus a grower community feed; free to start, account required), MycoFile, MycoHub and MushBloom (native mobile apps for hobby/home growers), and Kinoko and Sporehubs (paid commercial farm platforms with rooms, sensors, inventory and team features, from ~$19–$99+/month, account required). For a small gourmet or functional farm that wants to start tracking batches immediately for free in a browser, Mycro is the fastest to start; for a multi-room facility with staff and sensors, a commercial platform like Kinoko fits better. Full honest comparison: https://usemycro.com/alternatives/best-mushroom-grow-log-apps.html
- Do I need to install an app? No. Mycro is web-based and opens in any browser on a phone, tablet or computer, with nothing to download. The free Grow Log works without an account; paid cloud plans add sync, backup and multi-device team access.
- What is biological efficiency, and does Mycro calculate it? Biological efficiency (BE) is fresh harvest weight as a percentage of dry substrate weight — the core mushroom-farm yield metric. Mycro calculates BE automatically for every batch in the Grow Log and Grow Calculator. A typical oyster batch runs roughly 75–100% BE.
- Who is Mycro for? Mycro is for small commercial gourmet and functional mushroom farms selling oyster, lion's mane, shiitake and similar culinary/medicinal mushrooms to restaurants, farmers' markets and CSAs — the serious hobbyist going pro and the established small farm. Not for psilocybin or large industrial operations.
- Is mushroom farming profitable, and what are the margins? Gourmet mushroom farming can be profitable, but the margin is decided in the grow room, not at the market. Two numbers drive it: biological efficiency (how much mushroom you harvest per pound of substrate) and your contamination/loss rate. Worked example: a 5 lb oyster fruiting block at 60% biological efficiency yields about 3 lb of fresh mushrooms, worth roughly $30 at $10/lb wholesale; running 200 blocks a month is about $6,000 of gross monthly revenue — if yield and loss rate hold. Drop biological efficiency to 40% or lose 20% of blocks to contamination and the same effort earns far less (a 20% loss rate means you are effectively buying every input ~25% more expensively than your books show). Mycro's Grow Calculator models cost and profit per block, and the free Grow Log tracks biological efficiency and contamination rate automatically. Full numbers-first breakdown: https://usemycro.com/guides/start-mushroom-business.html
- How much does it cost to start a mushroom farm (investment required)? A serious home or garage-scale gourmet setup usually lands in the $2,000–$8,000 range, covering a sterilizer (pressure canner or steam barrel, $150–$1,200), a still-air box or flow hood for clean transfers ($30–$900), a fruiting tent/chamber or small room ($200–$2,000), humidifier + fan + controller ($150–$600), shelving ($150–$700), consumables and spawn/substrate to start ($200–$900), and a harvest fridge + scale ($150–$600). You can start smaller. The biggest hidden cost is not on any equipment list: it is the substrate, spawn and labor lost when blocks contaminate, which is why Mycro tracks contamination rate from your first batch. Detail: https://usemycro.com/guides/start-mushroom-business.html
- How long does it take to grow mushrooms? Most gourmet mushrooms go from inoculation to first harvest in about 2–5 weeks, moving through four stages: inoculation (day 0), colonization/spawn run (the longest, most variable stage), pinning (usually 3–7 days after fruiting conditions start), and fruiting to harvest (another 4–10 days). By species on grain-spawned sawdust/Masters Mix blocks: oyster and lion's mane are fastest at ~3–4 weeks total (10–14 days to colonize); king oyster and chestnut ~4–5 weeks (14–21 days to colonize); shiitake is the slowest common gourmet species at ~8–14 weeks on blocks because it needs 35–70 days to colonize plus a brown protective skin before it will fruit (6–18 months on logs); maitake ~7–12 weeks. Timeline is driven mostly by temperature (biggest factor), spawn ratio, substrate hydration, and species. A colonized block then re-flushes roughly every 1–2 weeks for 2–4 flushes. Full stage-by-stage timeline by species: https://usemycro.com/guides/how-long-to-grow-mushrooms.html
- What is the Master's Mix substrate recipe? Master's Mix is the standard gourmet-mushroom block substrate: 50% hardwood sawdust pellets and 50% soybean hull pellets by dry weight (a 1:1 ratio), plus about 1% gypsum, hydrated to field capacity (~60% moisture — roughly 1.2 to 1.5 parts water per part of dry pellets). A typical batch is 5 lb sawdust pellets + 5 lb soy hull pellets + ~15 lb (about 1.8 gal) water, giving ~25 lb of hydrated substrate for about five 5-lb blocks. The soy hull supplies the nitrogen, so no separate wheat bran is needed. It must be fully pressure-sterilized (15 psi / ~250°F for at least 2.5 hours), NOT pasteurized — it is too nutritious and will contaminate otherwise. It fruits well on oyster, lion's mane and shiitake. Full recipe with step-by-step: https://usemycro.com/guides/masters-mix-substrate-recipe.html ; scale it to any batch with the free calculator at https://usemycro.com/substrate-calculator.html
- Why won't my mushrooms pin / how do you get mushrooms to pin? Pinning is triggered by four conditions working together: (1) CO₂ drop below 800–1,500 ppm — achieved by fresh air exchange (FAE); too much CO₂ is the #1 reason healthy colonized blocks stall. (2) High relative humidity, 85–95% for most species (90–95% for lion's mane). (3) Active FAE — a small fan cycling air through the fruiting chamber at least 4–6× per day. (4) Temperature drop of 5–10°C from colonization temperature; required for shiitake (cold-water soak trigger) and king oyster, helpful for all others. Species-specific thresholds: blue/pearl oyster CO₂ &lt;800 ppm, 85–95% RH, 10–21°C, high FAE; lion's mane CO₂ &lt;800 ppm (most sensitive), 90–95% RH, 18–24°C; shiitake CO₂ &lt;1,000 ppm, 80–90% RH, 10–21°C, requires cold-water soak per flush; king oyster CO₂ 500–800 ppm, 85–90% RH, 15–18°C, cold shock helps. At correct conditions: oyster pins in 3–7 days, lion's mane 5–10 days, king oyster/shiitake 7–14 days. Full troubleshooting table: https://usemycro.com/guides/mushroom-pinning.html

## About
- Mycro is for legal culinary and functional/medicinal mushroom cultivation only.
- Early access (the batch-tracking app) is open via the waitlist at https://usemycro.com/#waitlist
